column1: What does Big O notation describe?
column2: How an algorithm's time or space requirement grows as the input size n increases, expressed for the worst case, ignoring constants and lower-order terms.

column1: Why do we drop constants and lower-order terms in Big O? (e.g. 3n²+5n+17)
column2: Because we care about the rate of growth for large n; the dominant term (n²) decides scalability, so 3n²+5n+17 simplifies to O(n²).

column1: O(1) — name and behaviour?
column2: Constant time: execution time does not change with n. Example: accessing array[i], stack push/pop.

column1: O(log n) — name and behaviour?
column2: Logarithmic time: grows very slowly; doubling n adds only one step. Example: binary search.

column1: O(n) — name and behaviour?
column2: Linear time: doubling n doubles the time. Example: linear search, a single loop over a list.

column1: O(n²) — name and behaviour?
column2: Polynomial (quadratic) time: doubling n quadruples the time. Example: bubble/insertion sort, nested loops.

column1: O(2ⁿ) — name and behaviour?
column2: Exponential time: adding one item doubles the time; intractable for large n. Example: naive recursive Fibonacci, brute-force travelling salesman.

column1: Order these from fastest to slowest growth: O(n²), O(1), O(2ⁿ), O(n), O(log n)
column2: O(1) < O(log n) < O(n) < O(n²) < O(2ⁿ).

column1: What are tractable and intractable problems?
column2: Tractable: solvable in polynomial time or better. Intractable: no known polynomial-time solution (only exponential or worse), so not solvable in reasonable time for large n.

column1: How does linear search work (one line)?
column2: Check each element in turn from the start until the target is found or the end is reached.

column1: Linear search — best, average, worst case, and does it need sorted data?
column2: Best O(1) (first element); average and worst O(n) (last or absent). It does NOT need sorted data.

column1: How does binary search work (one line)?
column2: Repeatedly examine the middle element of a sorted list and discard the half that cannot contain the target, halving the search space each pass.

column1: Binary search — precondition?
column2: The list must be sorted.

column1: Binary search — best, average, worst case?
column2: Best O(1) (target is the first middle); average and worst O(log n) (search space halves each step).

column1: Big O of binary search?
column2: O(log n).

column1: When does linear search beat binary search?
column2: When data is unsorted and searched only once: a single O(n) linear search is cheaper than sorting first (O(n log n)) then binary searching. Binary search pays off when the same sorted data is searched many times.

column1: How does bubble sort work (one line)?
column2: Repeatedly step through the list comparing adjacent pairs and swapping any in the wrong order, so the largest element bubbles to the end each pass.

column1: Bubble sort — best, average, worst, space?
column2: Best O(n) (already sorted, with swap flag); average and worst O(n²); space O(1).

column1: Why is bubble sort's best case O(n) only with a swap flag?
column2: The flag detects a pass with no swaps and stops early; without it bubble sort is O(n²) even on a sorted list.

column1: How does insertion sort work (one line)?
column2: Build a sorted section at the front by taking the next element and inserting it into its correct position, shifting larger elements right.

column1: Insertion sort — best, average, worst, space, and when to use?
column2: Best O(n) (nearly sorted); average and worst O(n²); space O(1). Good for small or nearly-sorted lists as it is in-place.

column1: How does merge sort work (one line)?
column2: Divide and conquer: recursively split the list in half until single elements, then merge the sublists back together in sorted order.

column1: Merge sort — best, average, worst, space?
column2: O(n log n) in all cases (best/average/worst); space O(n) for the temporary merge arrays.

column1: Why does merge sort guarantee O(n log n)?
column2: It always splits the list exactly in half, so the depth of splitting is log n and each level does O(n) merging work, regardless of input order.

column1: How does quick sort work (one line)?
column2: Divide and conquer: choose a pivot, partition items smaller to its left and larger to its right, then recursively quicksort each partition.

column1: Quick sort — best, average, worst, space?
column2: Best and average O(n log n); worst O(n²); space O(log n) (recursion stack).

column1: When does quick sort hit its O(n²) worst case?
column2: When the pivot repeatedly splits the list unevenly, e.g. always picking the smallest/largest element on already-sorted data.

column1: Merge sort vs quick sort — key trade-off?
column2: Merge sort guarantees O(n log n) but needs O(n) extra space; quick sort sorts in place (O(log n) space) and is usually fastest, but degrades to O(n²) in the worst case.

column1: Which standard sort is not generally stable?
column2: Quick sort. Bubble, insertion and merge sort are stable.

column1: Stack — what does LIFO mean and Big O of operations?
column2: Last In, First Out: items added/removed at the top. push, pop, peek, isEmpty are all O(1).

column1: Queue — FIFO meaning, Big O, and what does a circular queue use MOD for?
column2: First In, First Out: added at the rear (enQueue), removed from the front (deQueue); both O(1). A circular queue uses MOD to wrap the pointers around the array and reuse freed space.

column1: Linked list — structure and Big O of traversal vs insert?
column2: Each node holds data and a pointer to the next; a start pointer marks the head, null marks the end. Traversal/search is O(n); insert/delete given the node is O(1) (just re-point).

column1: Pre-order traversal — order and use?
column2: Node, Left, Right. Used to copy a tree / produce prefix (Polish) expressions.

column1: In-order traversal — order and use?
column2: Left, Node, Right. Outputs a binary search tree in ascending sorted order.

column1: Post-order traversal — order and use?
column2: Left, Right, Node. Used to delete a tree and to evaluate Reverse Polish (postfix) expressions. This is the depth-first traversal named in the spec.

column1: What is breadth-first traversal (BFS) and which structure does it use?
column2: Visit nodes level by level, left to right, using a queue. For the tree 50:(30:(20,40),70:(60,80)) → 50,30,70,20,40,60,80.

column1: Which data structure does depth-first use vs breadth-first?
column2: Depth-first uses a stack (or recursion's call stack); breadth-first uses a queue.

column1: Binary search tree — search/insert Big O?
column2: O(log n) average on a balanced tree; O(n) worst case on a degenerate/unbalanced tree. Traversal visits every node: O(n).

column1: What does Dijkstra's algorithm find?
column2: The shortest path from a start node to all other nodes in a weighted graph with non-negative edge weights.

column1: Dijkstra — one-line method?
column2: Keep tentative distances; repeatedly visit the unvisited node with the smallest tentative distance, relax its neighbours, then mark it visited (final).

column1: Dijkstra — complexity?
column2: O(n²) with a simple distance array; O((V+E) log V) with a priority queue (binary heap).

column1: What is A* and how does it differ from Dijkstra?
column2: A* is an informed extension of Dijkstra for a single start and single goal: it adds a heuristic estimate of the remaining cost, so it searches towards the goal and usually explores fewer nodes.

column1: A* — what is the cost function f(n)?
column2: f(n) = g(n) + h(n), where g(n) is the actual cost from the start and h(n) is the heuristic estimate of the remaining cost to the goal. A* expands the node with the lowest f(n).

column1: What is an admissible heuristic and why does it matter?
column2: One that never overestimates the true remaining cost. A* is guaranteed to find the optimal path only if the heuristic is admissible.

column1: What happens to A* if h(n) = 0 for all nodes?
column2: It behaves identically to Dijkstra's algorithm (searching outward in all directions).
